project_overview.project_development_objective: Enable sustainable recovery and restore lifeline services and critical infrastructure in conflict-affected areas of Lebanon
project_overview.total_financing: US$250 million IBRD loan (with US$1 billion total appraised project scope and US$750 million financing gap)
project_overview.beneficiaries: 1.2 million affected people living in priority conflict-affected districts; Lebanese economy and environment; local industries supporting construction

primary_locations: Other conflict-affected areas across Lebanese territory impacted by October 2023-December 2024 conflict
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on conflict-affected areas
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - project explicitly targets areas affected by 2023-24 conflict; Lebanon classified as fragile state with ongoing conflict

key_components.description: Safe and sustainable rubble management through circular economy approach (sorting, recycling, reuse of materials); hazardous waste management; securing damaged structures; cultural heritage protection; decommissioning IDP centers; rapid road access restoration. Includes rubble recycling and other priority response measures.
key_components.financing: US$80 million total (US$50 million IBRD available)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Rapid Recovery of Public Lifeline Services and Public Critical Infrastructure
key_components.description: Multi-sectoral area-based restoration of critical infrastructure and lifeline services (water, energy, health, education, transport, municipal services, communications) through repairs and alternative service delivery mechanisms. Prioritized by damage intensity, population, and economic potential. Includes temporary facilities, mobile clinics/classrooms, equipment replacement, vehicle replacement.
key_components.financing: US$420 million total (US$175 million IBRD available)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Sustainable and Robust Reconstruction of Public Critical Infrastructure and Public Lifeline Services
key_components.description: Two subcomponents: 3a) Building pipeline of execution-ready reconstruction investments through feasibility studies, designs, environmental/social studies, and exploration of private sector financing options; 3b) Infrastructure reconstruction works using climate and disaster-resistant designs. Incorporates Build Back Better approach with climate resilience, energy efficiency, water efficiency, universal accessibility, and seismic resistance.
key_components.financing: US$475 million total (US$15 million IBRD available for designs; US$0 for works in initial financing)

key_components.description: Support for Project Management Unit within CDR including monitoring and evaluation, reporting, procurement, financial management, environmental and social management, grievance redress, citizen engagement, communications, third-party monitoring, and CDR reform implementation (business process reengineering, digitalization).
key_components.financing: US$25 million total (US$10 million IBRD available)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Council for Development and Reconstruction (CDR) with overall leadership from Ministry of Public Works and Transport (MoPWT); coordination with Ministries of Energy and Water, Education and Higher Education, Social Affairs, Public Health, and Environment
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Prime Minister and Minister of Finance provide strategic guidance; MoPWT has overall oversight; CDR implements with PMU reporting to CDR President and Board; coordination with line ministries through focal points; regular inter-ministerial coordination meetings; stakeholder consultation sessions in prioritized areas
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: WB Procurement Regulations for IPF (February 2025); WB Guidance on Procurement in Situations of Urgent Need; STEP system for tracking; Hands-on Expanded Implementation Support (HEIS) from WB Senior Procurement Specialist; advance procurement allowed; prior review for contracts over US$5M (works), US$2M (goods), US$400K (consultancy firms), US$200K (individual consultants); direct payment and reimbursement methods; early market engagement; fair contractual terms with adjustment formulas
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PMU responsible for M&E with dedicated M&E officers; quarterly implementation progress reports; Third-Party Monitoring Agency (TPMA) for verification of activities and expenditures through site visits, satellite imagery, surveys; enhanced WB supervision with doubled missions for first two years; international lender's engineering firm for compliance due diligence; external auditor for annual financial statements; results framework with PDO and intermediate indicators; beneficiary feedback mechanism; grievance redress mechanism

identified_risks.risk_description: Recent political developments (new president, PM, government) present opportunity but past challenges with loan agreement signing and parliamentary ratification have caused delays; geopolitical risks from Middle East conflict may impede implementation in border areas; political instability could affect government commitment
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong government ownership and commitment; close follow-up with borrower at all levels; rapid repair prioritized in areas most urgent for livelihood restoration; exclusion of disputed territories from eligible areas; coordination with other financiers to maintain momentum

identified_risks.risk_description: Ongoing economic crisis with 7.1% contraction expected in 2024; triple-digit inflation (now easing to double digits); Lebanese pound lost 98% of value; dollarized cash-based economy; reconstruction activity may drive up construction material and labor prices; currency depreciation risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project contracts denominated in US dollars; Designated Account in US$; contingent financing set aside for anticipated price increases; focus on rapid recovery to restore economic activity; monitoring of macroeconomic indicators

identified_risks.risk_description: Multi-sectoral project with pervasive deficits in all covered sectors prior to conflict; inadequate rubble management in 2006 did not meet international standards; limited MoE capacity to enforce environmental obligations; outdated building and urban regulations; need for green and disaster-resistant reconstruction standards
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: MoE issued compulsory environmental guidance (Circulars 6/1 and 1/1) that are contractually binding; project can finance preparation of strategies, plans, and regulations; technical assistance for building standards; incorporation of international standards (Eurocode 8 for seismic resistance, LEED/BREEAM for energy efficiency)

identified_risks.risk_description: Multi-sectoral coverage creates coordination and communication risks across ministries and subnational levels; gap between available financing (US$250M) and needs (US$1B) requires transparent prioritization; complexity of integrated area-based approach
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Clear prioritization criteria established (damage intensity, population, economic potential); PMU leadership supports cross-ministerial dialogue; TPMA monitors progress and verifies activities; WB enhanced supervision; flexible project design allows adjustment as financing materializes

identified_risks.risk_description: CDR has experience with WB projects but limited capacity for project of this scale; inefficient decision-making processes (procurement approval takes 9 months, bid evaluation 2-8 months); limited human resources; need for environmental and social specialists
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Autonomous PMU reporting directly to CDR President; CDR reform measures in Policy Letter including business process reengineering, digitalization, delegation of decision-making; FM consultant hired within one month of effectiveness; dedicated environmental, social, and OHS specialists appointed; HEIS support from WB; enhanced WB supervision

identified_risks.risk_description: High perception of corruption in country; WB team security restrictions may limit physical verification; CDR capacity constraints in monitoring and controls; substantial works and specialized services require enhanced monitoring; unstable exchange rate impacts; Court of Accounts has limited capacity to audit project
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: TPMA engagement within one month of effectiveness; FM Manual as part of POM; FM consultant hired within one month; quarterly interim financial reports within 45 days; external auditor acceptable to WB; contracts in US$; DA in US$; international lender's engineering firm for ex-post verification and compliance due diligence; STEP system for AML/CFT detection; enhanced WB supervision with quarterly verification reports

identified_risks.risk_description: High procurement risk despite CDR's generally satisfactory capacity; market instability and price fluctuations; exchange rate volatility; complex multisectoral activities; coordination challenges with multiple stakeholders; contractor availability uncertain; internal CDR bureaucratic bottlenecks; absence of green procurement policies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: HEIS support from WB Senior Procurement Specialist; TPMA for contract execution monitoring; qualified supervision consultants; early market engagement and market research; fair contractual terms with adjustment formulas; business process reengineering of CDR procurement; STEP system training; coordination mechanisms in POM; technical support for green procurement; communication strategy to attract contractors

identified_risks.risk_description: Environmental risks: ERW and worker health/safety during rubble sorting; inadequate waste management at temporary sites; air emissions, dust, noise during operations; potential impacts to flora/fauna from improper disposal. Social risks: labor and OHS issues; social exclusion from prioritization; information disclosure gaps; sexual harassment and exploitation risks; vulnerable groups may be excluded
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Site-specific ESIAs and ESMPs; OHS measures and plans; traffic and road safety measures; waste management plans; security risk management; biodiversity conservation measures; chance find procedures; Stakeholder Engagement Plan with robust grievance mechanism; SEA/SH response plan; labor management procedures; gender-disaggregated data collection; awareness campaigns; beneficiary feedback mechanism; community consultations in prioritized areas

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple stakeholders requiring effective coordination; interface risks between MoE, MoPWT, CDR, and rubble removal contractors; substantial interest from NGOs, academics, media on rubble management; risk of civil society concerns about prioritized areas; coordination challenges across horizontal and vertical levels
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Transparent prioritization mechanism based on agreed criteria; Stakeholder Engagement Plan with consultation sessions; grievance redress mechanism; communications strategy; citizen engagement platform; beneficiary feedback mechanism; regular stakeholder coordination meetings; clear communication on recovery progress

identified_risks.risk_description: US$750 million financing gap between appraised needs (US$1B) and available IBRD financing (US$250M); if gap not closed, project targets will be scaled down; unmet beneficiary expectations if full scope not implemented
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Proactive efforts to identify additional financing from development partners; WB convening power to crowd in additional resources; flexible project design allows absorption of additional financing; project restructuring planned if financing gap remains; clear communication on phased implementation; prioritization of highest-impact activities with available financing

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance mechanism to be established by PMU with service standards; beneficiary feedback mechanism (BFM) to track closure of feedback/query loop; existing communication channels of line ministries; WB Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available; WB Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) available; grievance indicator in results framework (95% of grievances addressed)
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender gap analysis conducted; conflict affected 11,000+ pregnant women; maternal mortality ratio increased from 18 (2010) to 20.6 (2020); conflict damaged 40 hospitals and 42 PHCCs affecting MCH services; project will repair and re-equip health facilities providing MCH services; mobile health clinics for MCH services; telemedicine services; strengthened supply chain for essential medicines; awareness campaigns on rehabilitated health services; dedicated indicator for pregnant women receiving antenatal care (target 130,000); broader support through restored education and social facilities; reduced household burdens through reliable water, electricity, mobility
vulnerable_groups: Internally displaced persons (900,000+ displaced during conflict, 100,000+ remaining displaced as of January 2025)
Poor and extremely poor (32% of Lebanese nationals, 44% of people living in Lebanon in poverty in 2024)
Syrian refugees (1.5 million hosted in Lebanon)
Women (disproportionately affected by disasters in labor participation, unemployment, asset losses)
Persons with disabilities (universal accessibility required in reconstructed buildings)
People in areas with lower economic activity (may perceive exclusion from prioritization)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for post-conflict recovery and reconstruction following 2023-24 conflict affecting Lebanon (October 8, 2023 - December 20, 2024); conflict-affected areas defined as territory impacted by this conflict; project supports Build Back Better approach incorporating social cohesion and economic transformation; transparent and accountable mechanisms embedded to rebuild trust in public institutions; area-based approach ensures no part or group of society left behind; exclusion of disputed territories from eligible areas; security clearance required (Lebanese Army clearance for ERW); geopolitical risks from Middle East conflict acknowledged and mitigated through prioritization of areas most urgent for livelihood restoration
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon classified as fragile state; overlapping multifaceted crises: 2011 Syrian conflict and refugee crisis (1.5M refugees), 2019 economic and financial crisis (40% GDP decline since 2019), 2020 Port of Beirut explosion, COVID-19 pandemic, 2023-24 conflict; macroeconomic crisis with 98% currency devaluation, triple-digit inflation (now easing), systemic banking failure, dollarized economy; poverty tripled from 12% (2012) to 32% (2024); pervasive deficits in public service delivery (22 hours/day electricity blackouts, water scarcity affecting 70% of population, inadequate SWM); weakening governance with high civil service attrition; human capital index declined; climate vulnerability (ranked 156/192 in climate adaptation readiness); seismic risk in region
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Explosive Remnants of War (ERW) anticipated in highly damaged areas; careful management required to reduce fatalities and injuries during rubble management; Lebanese Army clearance required for ERW before investment in areas; unmanaged human remains make areas ineligible; security conditions may restrict WB team visits for physical verification; geopolitical risks from Middle East conflict may impede implementation in border areas; security risk management measures included in ESMPs; occupational health and safety measures mandatory for contractors
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - 900,000+ people displaced during 2023-24 conflict; 100,000+ people remaining displaced as of January 2025; project includes measures to decommission centers for internally displaced persons and revert buildings to original use (e.g., schools); 1.5 million Syrian refugees hosted in Lebanon straining public services; project supports restoration of lifeline services critical for both displaced persons and refugee populations; housing sector damaged with 162,900 housing units (10% of total stock) sustaining damage; housing repairs expected challenging for 35% of poor who own homes; rental prices in Beirut increased from US$300 to US$1,400/month in 2024; project includes housing reconstruction planning and exploration of private sector financing options
